chunk_summary: Guidelines for selecting enteral access devices based on duration and aspiration risk, emphasizing nasogastric, gastrostomy, and postpyloric options tailored to patient needs and local resources.
learning_objectives: Understand criteria for selecting enteral access devices based on duration of support and aspiration risk.
Differentiate between nasogastric, orogastric, gastrostomy, and postpyloric feeding options.
Recognize the importance of supply stability and caregiver capacity in access planning.
Learn the role of early consultation with gastroenterology or surgery teams in optimizing enteral nutrition.
key_facts: Nasogastric (NG) tubes are suitable for short-term support (<4–6 weeks) with manageable aspiration risk.
Orogastric (OG) tubes are preferred in neonates and preterm infants to reduce nasal trauma and airway resistance.
Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) is the first choice for long-term enteral support when anatomy and expertise permit.
Postpyloric feeding (e.g., nasojejunal or jejunostomy) is recommended for severe reflux, recurrent aspiration pneumonia, or poor gastric emptying.
Postpyloric feeding often requires continuous delivery and explicit discharge planning.
Indian practice emphasizes supply stability and caregiver capacity for safe maintenance of enteral access devices.
reference_quote: Enteral access choice should start with anticipated duration and aspiration risk, not formula preference.
document_key_topics: Enteral access device selection by duration
Aspiration risk and feeding route decisions
